text: The laparoscopic view reveals the mass in the rectosigmoid colon, visibly attached to the dome of the bladder.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to dissect the mesorectal tissue, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon opens the retroperitoneal mesenteric window, isolating both the inferior mesenteric artery and vein, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates the isolated inferior mesenteric artery with two hemoclips and then cuts it, completing the vascular control step in the laparoscopic lower anterior resection.
The surgeon ligates the mesenteric vein using hemoclips and then cuts it with an energy device, completing the isolation of the vein within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection on the lateral side by incising the white line of Toldt, further mobilizing the colon from its lateral attachments.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon, freeing it from surrounding tissues and structures.
The surgeon includes the dome of the bladder, which is visibly invaded by cancer, into the specimen to ensure safe surgical margins.
The surgeon transects the rectum using surgical staplers, completing the division of the rectal tissue.
The surgeon repairs the superior wall of the bladder using a single layer continuous suture, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated and secured.
The surgeon performs colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the transected ends of the colon and rectum.
The surgeon performs an air leak test on the repaired bladder, confirming the absence of air leaks in the suture line.
